elements.sent-2411-001: Diffusion models have shown remarkable success in text-to-image generation, making alignment methods for these models increasingly important.
elements.sent-2411-002: A key challenge is the sparsity of preference labels, which are typically available only at the terminal of denoising trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-003: This raises the issue of how to assign credit across denoising steps based on these sparse labels.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose Denoised Distribution Estimation (\algoname), a novel method for credit assignment.
elements.sent-2411-005: Unlike previous approaches that rely on auxiliary models or hand-crafted schemes, \algoname derives its strategy more explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed \algoname directly estimates the terminal denoised distribution from the perspective of each step.
elements.sent-2411-007: It is equipped with two estimation strategies and capable of representing the entire denoising trajectory with a single model inference.
elements.sent-2411-008: Theoretically and empirically, we show that \algoname prioritizes optimizing the middle part of the denoising trajectory, resulting in a novel and effective credit assignment scheme.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves superior performance, both quantitatively and qualitatively.
elements.sent-2411-010: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in text-to-image generation \cite{NeurIPS20Ho, ICLR21Songddim, CVPR22Rombach .}
elements.sent-2411-011: A key challenge in generative modeling is alignment \cite{CoRR24Liu, CoRR23Wang , which focuses on improving a model's ability to better align with human preferences.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Alignment training has been extensively explored in the context of large language models \cite{CoRR20Stiennon, CoRR19Ziegler, NeurIPS22Ouyang .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Initially driven by Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), alignment techniques have evolved to include approaches such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) \cite{NeurIPS23Rafailov .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The latter has gained significant attraction, inspiring a series of subsequent studies \cite{ICML24Chen, CoRR23RRHFYuan, AISTATS24Azar .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Despite the variety of emerging approaches, few studies have attempted to adapt Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to text-to-image diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-016: The primary challenge lies in the sparsity of preference labels.
elements.sent-2411-017: Specifically, human evaluators can only provide preference labels for the final noiseless output of generative models.
elements.sent-2411-018: In contrast, diffusion models generate images progressively, producing large numbers of noisy intermediate results that are difficult to label.
elements.sent-2411-019: So the question is: How can credit be assigned across the denoising steps when preference labels are only available at the terminal of the denoising trajectory? Recent studies have explored two main approaches to address this challenge.
elements.sent-2411-020: One approach relies on auxiliary models, such as reward models \cite{CoRR23DPOK, ICLR24Black or noisy evaluators \cite{CoRR24Liang .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: These models essentially learn a weighting function to adaptively assign credit from the final output to each denoising step.
elements.sent-2411-022: However, this approach introduces additional training complexity, undermining the simplicity of DPO.
elements.sent-2411-023: The second approach requires no auxiliary models, maintaining the simplicity of DPO but limiting the ability to perform effective credit assignments.
elements.sent-2411-024: These methods typically rely on simple strategies, such as uniform assignment \cite{CVPR24Yang, CVPR24Wallace or discounted assignment \cite{ICML24Yang (\ie placing more weight on the initial denoising steps), which may restrict the alignment potential of the model.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper, we propose Denoised Distribution Estimation, a DPO method without auxiliary models for diffusion model alignment training.
elements.sent-2411-026: Instead of directly designing a hand-crafted credit assignment scheme, \algoname advocates deriving the assignment mechanism via explicit estimation to the terminal denoised distribution from the perspective of each step.
elements.sent-2411-027: To this end, we propose two estimation strategies: stepwise and single-shot estimation.
elements.sent-2411-028: The stepwise estimation uses the ground-truth conditional distribution ($q(x_{t-1 |x_t, x_0)$) to estimate the model distribution ($p_\theta(x_{t-1 |x_t)$), while the single-shot estimation utilizes DDIM modeling to directly estimate the terminal distribution ($p_\theta(\hat{x _0|x_t)$) based on an intermediate noisy latent state.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: By integrating these two estimation strategies, we can evaluate the entire denoising trajectory with a single model inference, which can then be leveraged during training.
elements.sent-2411-030: We analyze our \algoname both theoretically and empirically, revealing that it effectively prioritizes different denoising steps.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, our estimation strategies introduce correction terms and step-specific coefficients, which lead to greater credit being assigned to the middle portion of the denoising trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-032: In other words, we naturally derive a novel credit assignment scheme by focusing solely on the estimation of the whole denoising trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-033: We evaluate our method on both SD15 and SDXL quantitatively and qualitatively.
elements.sent-2411-034: The results demonstrate that compared with all other methods without auxiliary models, our \algoname achieves state-of-the-art performance.
elements.sent-2411-035: Specifically, our \algoname improves performance metrics of SD15 and SDXL by 3.3\%$\sim$6.7\% and 1.0\%$\sim$3.1\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-036: Our contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We introduce \algoname, a novel DPO method tailored for diffusion models without any auxiliary model.
elements.sent-2411-038: By directly estimating the terminal denoised distribution from the perspective of each step, \algoname naturally derives a novel credit assignment scheme for sparse preference labels.
elements.sent-2411-040: Theoretical and empirical analysis reveals that these strategies prioritize optimizing middle steps in denoising trajectories, which is different from existing uniform or discounted schemes.
elements.sent-2411-041: Experimental results show that \algoname achieves state-of-the-art performance both quantitatively and qualitatively, compared to existing methods without auxiliary models.

elements.sent-2411-053: We introduce our Denoised Distribution Estimation (\algoname) method, which adapts Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-054: The key challenge is the sparsity of preference labels.
elements.sent-2411-055: These labels are only tagged on the terminal of denoising trajectories (\ie noiseless images $x_0^w \succ x^l_0$), leaving these intermediate results ($x_t, \forall t \ne 0$) unlabeled.
elements.sent-2411-056: Previous research has addressed this issue by either utilizing auxiliary models, such as reward model \cite{CoRR23DPOK, ICLR24Black , or employing hand-crafted assignment schemes, including uniform \cite{CVPR24Yang, CVPR24Wallace and discounted strategies \cite{ICML24Yang .}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Our approach falls into the second category (without auxiliary models), aiming to preserve the simplicity inherent in DPO training.
elements.sent-2411-058: Distinct from existing methods that directly target the assignment scheme design, we advocate to investigate the final impact from the perspective of each step.

elements.sent-2411-061: Training feasibility limits the number of model inferences that can be executed.
elements.sent-2411-062: Consequently, the final denoised distribution must be estimated with a reduced number of inferences, or preferably, in a single inference.

elements.sent-2411-074: Unlike previous methods where the denoising process is modeled as a Markov sequential decision problem, our \algoname explicitly estimates the learned denoised distribution.

elements.sent-2411-109: We find that two estimation strategies of our \algoname method naturally introduce a credit assignment scheme that prioritizes the optimization of the middle steps of denoising trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-110: Stepwise and single-shot estimation weakens the optimization of steps around $0$ and $T$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-111: The stepwise estimation strategy introduces correction terms to the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-112: A larger correction term pushes the MSE terms closer into the gradient saturation region of the negative log-likelihood function, shown in \figref{subfig:log-sigmoid , reducing the optimization effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-113: In practice, we observe that the correction term will quickly converge, with its value diminishing for larger steps (see \secref{subsec:correction for more details).}
elements.sent-2411-114: This is because when the sampled $t$ is closer to time steps around $0$ (\ie noiseless image), the segment $T\rightarrow t$ becomes longer, leading to higher estimation errors and larger correction terms.
elements.sent-2411-115: Consequently, this pushes the whole term into the gradient saturation region.
elements.sent-2411-116: Essentially, the stepwise estimation weakens the optimization of steps around $0$.
elements.sent-2411-117: The single-shot estimation strategy introduces weight coefficients to the predicted noise due to the DDIM modeling.
elements.sent-2411-118: These coefficients can be directly computed and increase with the rise in $t$, which is shown in \figref{subfig:coeffs .}
elements.sent-2411-119: Larger coefficients magnify even a slight difference between the training and the reference model, pushing the term into the saturation region of the negative log-sigmoid function, as shown in \figref{subfig:log-sigmoid .}
elements.sent-2411-120: Consequently, the single-shot estimation weakens the optimization for steps near $T$, where the coefficients are maximal.
elements.sent-2411-121: It is critical to note that larger coefficients do not necessarily bring stronger optimization, as they influence both the training and reference model terms.
elements.sent-2411-122: In summary, by combining both estimation strategies, our method weakens the optimization at both ends of the denoising trajectory, effectively strengthening the optimization of the middle part.
elements.sent-2411-123: We refer to this as the ``prioritization property'' of our \algoname method.
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Assigning credits more on the middle part is reasonable.
elements.sent-2411-124: The prioritization property distinguishes our method from existing approaches.
elements.sent-2411-125: We initiate our comparison with the uniform credit assignment scheme \cite{CVPR24Wallace, CVPR24Yang , which defines the loss function as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{\text{Uni}} = \mathbb{E}_{x^w, x^l\sim \mathcal{D}}[ -\log \sigma(\beta\log\frac{p_\theta(x_{t-1}^w|x_t^w)}{p_{ref}(x_{t-1}^w|x_t^w)} - \beta\log \frac{p_\theta(x_{t-1}^l|x_t^l)}{p_{ref}(x_{t-1}^l|x_t^l)}]. \end{aligned} \label{equ:base-loss}\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-195: RLHF has been recently utilized in generative models, particularly large language models \cite{NeurIPS22Ouyang, CoRR20Stiennon, CoRR19Ziegler .}
elements.sent-2411-196: A typical RLHF framework trains a reward model using preference data, and then applies a proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm \cite{CoRR17Schulman to optimize the policy (\ie, the generative model).}
elements.sent-2411-197: One key drawback of this approach is the high computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-198: To address this issue, Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) was proposed \cite{NeurIPS23Rafailov based on the connection between the optimal policy and the reward model.}
elements.sent-2411-199: This approach has gained significant attention and has been explored in various studies \cite{ICML24Chen, CoRR23RRHFYuan, AISTATS24Azar .}
elements.sent-2411-200: However, extending DPO to diffusion models remains an underexplored area.
elements.sent-2411-201: By learning the inverse process (score matching \cite{ICLR21Songsm ), these models can generate images from randomly sampled noise.}
elements.sent-2411-202: Techniques like latent space conversion \cite{CVPR22Rombach and DDIM sampling \cite{ICLR21Songddim further enhance both the efficiency and effectiveness of the generation process.}}
elements.sent-2411-203: Recent studies have explored applying the Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) algorithm to align diffusion models \cite{CoRR23DPOK, ICLR24Black, CoRR24Liang, CVPR24Yang, CVPR24Wallace, ICML24Yang .}
elements.sent-2411-204: One of the main challenges in applying DPO to diffusion models is the sparsity of preference labels.
elements.sent-2411-205: The credit assignment strategy in these methods forms two major streams of thought.
elements.sent-2411-206: The first stream relies on auxiliary models.
elements.sent-2411-207: Some approaches, like those in \cite{ICLR24Black, CoRR23DPOK , train a reward model and apply reinforcement learning for optimization.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Others, such as \cite{CoRR24Liang , train an evaluator for noisy latents, similar to classifier-guided methods \cite{NeurIPS21Dhariwal that use classifiers on noisy images.}}
elements.sent-2411-209: While effective, these methods introduce training complexity, which detracts from the simplicity of DPO.
elements.sent-2411-210: The second stream avoids auxiliary models, but this makes credit assignment across denoising steps more challenging.
elements.sent-2411-211: For example, \cite{CVPR24Wallace, CVPR24Yang proposes a rough approximation to uniformly optimize all steps, while \cite{ICML24Yang introduces discounted factors, handcrafting a weighting function that prioritizes early denoising steps.}}
elements.sent-2411-212: However, these methods rely on heuristics and fail to estimate the impact of each step with sufficient accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-213: Our method falls into the second stream which is free from the complexity of auxiliary models.
elements.sent-2411-214: Also, it attempts to avoid the roughly hand-crafted credit assignment scheme and derives a novel scheme by revealing the impact on the terminal denoised distribution of each step.
elements.sent-2411-215: In this paper, we introduce Denoised Distribution Estimation (\algoname), a novel direct preference optimization method tailored for diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-216: Our \algoname addresses the challenge of credit assignment across denoising steps, an issue stemming from the sparsity of preference labels, by leveraging an enhanced insight into the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-217: Our analysis reveals that these two strategies implicitly prioritize the optimization of intermediate steps within the denoising trajectory, which is a key distinction from existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-218: Experimental evaluations show that our approach outperforms previous methods.
elements.sent-2411-219: It generates images with superior metrics of CLIP, HPS, and PS and achieves better alignment of human preference in terms of detail, text-image coherence, and structures.

elements.sent-2411-256: Diffusion models have shown remarkable success in text-to-image generation, making preference alignment for these models increasingly important.
elements.sent-2411-257: The preference labels are typically available only at the terminal of denoising trajectories, which poses challenges in optimizing the intermediate denoising steps.
elements.sent-2411-258: In this paper, we propose to conduct Denoised Distribution Estimation (DDE) that explicitly connects intermediate steps to the terminal denoised distribution.
elements.sent-2411-259: Therefore, preference labels can be used for the entire trajectory optimization.
elements.sent-2411-260: To this end, we design two estimation strategies for our DDE.
elements.sent-2411-261: The first is stepwise estimation, which utilizes the conditional denoised distribution to estimate the model denoised distribution.
elements.sent-2411-262: The second is single-shot estimation, which converts the model output into the terminal denoised distribution via DDIM modeling.
elements.sent-2411-263: Analytically and empirically, we reveal that DDE equipped with two estimation strategies naturally derives a novel credit assignment scheme that prioritizes optimizing the middle part of the denoising trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-264: Initially driven by Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) \cite{ICLR24Lightman , alignment techniques have evolved to many other approaches \cite{CoRR23RRHFYuan, ACL24Amini, NeurIPS24Wu such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) \cite{NeurIPS23Rafailov, AISTATS24Azar, NeurIPS24Meng .}}}
elements.sent-2411-265: Despite the variety of emerging approaches, few studies have attempted to adapt DPO to text-to-image diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-266: The primary challenge lies in the terminal-only issue of preference labels.

elements.sent-2411-268: This raises the question: How to optimize each intermediate denoising step with terminal preference labels only? Recent studies tend to solve this terminal-only issue from the perspective of credit assignment \cite{ICLR16Lillicrap, Arxiv23Mnih .}
elements.sent-2411-269: These methods can be mainly divided into two categories.

elements.sent-2411-272: The other requires hand-craft schemes, maintaining the simplicity of DPO but limiting the ability to perform effective credit assignments.

elements.figure-2411-016: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.2cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0.5cm} \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/framework.pdf} \caption{ Overall framework of \algoname. The training process is outlined as follows: 1) Sample random noises ($x_T^w, x_T^l$), winning and losing samples ($x_0^w, x_0^l$) and a denoising step $t$; 2) Conduct stepwise estimation from $T\rightarrow t$. By using $\exp\{r_k\} q(x_{k}|x_{k+1}, x_0)$ as an estimation to $p_\theta(x_k|x_{k+1})$ for all $t\le k \le T-1$, the cumulative product of denoising steps from $T$ to $t$ is estimated as $\exp\{\sum_{k=t}^{T-1} r_k\} q(x_t|x_0)$; 3) Apply single-shot estimation from $t \rightarrow 0$. By using DDIM, $p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)$ is converted to $p_\theta(\hat{x}_{0}|x_t)$ with one single model calculation; 4) Leverage the preference label on $x_0$ for training. Additionally, in step 3, $p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)$ is used to calculate non-gradient calibration coefficients $r_{t-1} = \log \frac{p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)}{q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0)}$. These coefficients are updated using an exponential moving average for subsequent iterations. } \label{fig:framework}
